It tells the story of Ji Zha (季札), a prince of the state of Wu, who was renowned for his wisdom and virtue. On a diplomatic mission to the state of Zheng, Ji Zha met the statesman Zichan (子产). Despite meeting for the first time, they felt an immediate connection, as if they were old friends. The idiom 一见如故 (一, one; 见, meet; 如, like; 故, old) captures this sense of instant rapport and familiarity. In modern usage, it describes situations where people feel an immediate bond upon first meeting, suggesting shared values or mutual understanding.

item.title: 两肋插刀 - Extreme loyalty
item.description: Commonly used in classical Chinese, the idiom 两肋插刀 (liǎng lèi chā dāo) originates from a tale involving the famed hero Qin Shubao during the late Sui Dynasty. As a loyal friend, Qin Shubao risked his life to save his comrades by deliberately leading law enforcement astray. The idiom literally means 'to insert knives into both sides,' symbolizing the willingness to endure extreme hardship for the sake of friendship. The characters 两 (liǎng) mean 'both,' 肋 (lèi) means 'ribs,' 插 (chā) means 'insert,' and 刀 (dāo) means 'knife.' In modern usage, this idiom describes someone who is willing to make great sacrifices for friends, emphasizing loyalty and selflessness. However, it also serves as a reminder to balance loyalty with discernment, ensuring that actions remain within moral and legal boundaries.

item.title: 情同手足 - Sibling-like bond
item.description: Commonly used in classical Chinese, the idiom 情同手足 (qíng tóng shǒu zú) vividly describes a relationship as close as that between one's hands (手) and feet (足). This imagery emphasizes the deep bond and mutual support akin to that of siblings. In modern usage, it is often applied to friendships or relationships where individuals share an unbreakable connection, akin to family. The idiom highlights the importance of loyalty and support in nurturing such profound relationships.

item.title: 肝脑涂地 - Ultimate sacrifice
item.description: The idiom 肝脑涂地 (gān nǎo tú dì) originates from the historical text 'Records of the Grand Historian' (史记) by Sima Qian (司马迁). It describes the aftermath of numerous battles during the rise of Liu Bang (刘邦), the founder of the Han Dynasty. Liu Bang's advisor, Lou Jing (娄敬), urged him to move the capital to Guanzhong, citing the devastation of war where people's 'livers and brains' (肝, liver; 脑, brain) were 'smeared on the ground' (涂地, smeared on the ground). This vivid imagery highlights the extreme sacrifices made during warfare. In modern usage, the idiom conveys a sense of unwavering loyalty and willingness to sacrifice everything, even life itself, for a cause or relationship.

item.title: 赤子之心 - Pure and sincere heart
item.description: The idiom 赤子之心 (chì zǐ zhī xīn) is commonly used in classical Chinese literature to describe a heart that is pure, innocent, and sincere, akin to that of a newborn child. The phrase originates from the teachings of Mencius (孟子), who emphasized the importance of maintaining one's original, untainted nature. In his work 《孟子·离娄下》, Mencius states that a truly great person is one who does not lose their 赤子之心. The characters 赤 (chì) mean 'red' or 'naked,' 子 (zǐ) means 'child,' and 之心 (zhī xīn) means 'heart.' Together, they paint a vivid image of a heart that is unblemished and genuine. In modern usage, this idiom is often employed to praise individuals who possess a child-like purity and goodness, remaining true to their innate virtues despite the complexities of the world.

item.title: 天伦之乐 - Family happiness
item.description: The idiom 天伦之乐 (tiān lún zhī lè) originates from the Tang dynasty, specifically from a work by the renowned poet Li Bai (李白). In his prose piece 'Spring Night Banquet in the Peach and Plum Garden' (春夜宴从弟桃花园序), Li Bai describes a joyful gathering with his brothers, celebrating familial bonds and the happiness they bring. The phrase '序天伦之乐事' refers to the enjoyment of family ties. The characters break down as follows: 天 (tiān) means 'heaven' or 'sky,' 伦 (lún) refers to 'relations' or 'ethics,' 之 (zhī) is a possessive particle, and 乐 (lè) means 'joy' or 'happiness.' In modern usage, this idiom highlights the joy and contentment found in harmonious family relationships, emphasizing the importance of cherishing time with loved ones.

item.title: 骨肉相连 - Inseparable family bond
item.description: Commonly used in classical Chinese, the idiom 骨肉相连 (gǔ ròu xiāng lián) literally translates to 'bones and flesh are connected.' It evokes the image of the deep, inseparable bond between family members, akin to the physical connection of bones and flesh in the human body. The characters 骨 (gǔ) meaning 'bone,' 肉 (ròu) meaning 'flesh,' 相 (xiāng) meaning 'mutually,' and 连 (lián) meaning 'connected,' collectively emphasize the idea of familial ties that are strong and enduring. In modern usage, this idiom is often employed to describe the profound and unbreakable relationships between close family members, highlighting the natural and inherent connections that bind them together.

item.title: 手足之情 - Brotherly affection
item.description: The idiom 手足之情 (shǒu zú zhī qíng) is commonly used in classical Chinese to describe the profound bond between siblings, akin to the inseparable relationship between one's hands (手) and feet (足). This expression emphasizes the deep emotional connection and mutual support that siblings share, both physically and spiritually. The idiom's imagery suggests that just as hands and feet are essential and work together harmoniously, so too do siblings rely on and support each other. In modern usage, 手足之情 is often invoked to highlight the importance of familial bonds and the irreplaceable nature of sibling relationships, extending sometimes to describe close friendships that resemble sibling ties.

item.title: 高风亮节 - Noble and steadfast
item.description: Commonly used in classical Chinese literature, the idiom 高风亮节 (gāo fēng liàng jié) praises individuals with noble character and unwavering integrity. The phrase is composed of two parts: '高风' (gāo fēng), meaning 'high moral character,' and '亮节' (liàng jié), meaning 'bright integrity.' This idiom is often used to commend those who demonstrate steadfast principles, even in challenging circumstances. Historically, it has been applied to figures such as Su Wu, who maintained his loyalty despite being detained by the Xiongnu, and Guo Ziyi, who recommended Li Guangbi despite personal grievances. In modern contexts, it is used to honor individuals or groups who exhibit moral fortitude and ethical conduct, such as during the 2020 Wuhan pandemic response, where it highlighted the resilience and character of the city's residents.

item.title: 正人君子 - Moral integrity
item.description: The idiom 正人君子 (zhèng rén jūn zǐ) is commonly used in classical Chinese literature to describe a person of upright character and high moral standards. The term is composed of two parts: 正人 (zhèng rén), meaning 'upright person,' and 君子 (jūn zǐ), which refers to a 'gentleman' or a person of noble conduct. Historically, 君子 was a term used to describe members of the educated and virtuous scholar-official class. This idiom is found in historical texts such as the 《旧唐书》 (Old Book of Tang) and 《新唐书》 (New Book of Tang), where it was used to praise individuals who were morally upright. In modern usage, however, it often carries a sarcastic tone, implying someone who appears virtuous but is hypocritical. This dual usage highlights the complexity of moral judgment in social and political contexts.

item.title: 德高望重 - Respected elder
item.description: The idiom 德高望重 (dé gāo wàng zhòng) is commonly used in classical Chinese literature to describe individuals of high moral character and great respect. The phrase originated from the historical context of the Jin Dynasty, as referenced in the works of 房玄龄 (Fang Xuanling) and later adapted into its current form. The idiom is often associated with figures like 富弼 (Fu Bi), a renowned statesman of the Northern Song Dynasty, who was known for his integrity and dedication to public service. The characters 德 (dé) mean 'virtue' or 'moral character,' 高 (gāo) means 'high,' 望 (wàng) refers to 'reputation' or 'esteem,' and 重 (zhòng) means 'weighty' or 'significant.' In modern usage, it is used to honor elders or leaders who are admired for their ethical standards and contributions to society.

item.title: 心胸开阔 - Open-mindedness
item.description: The idiom 心胸开阔 (xīn xiōng kāi kuò) is commonly used in classical Chinese to describe a person with a broad mind and generous spirit. It is composed of two parts: 心胸 (xīn xiōng), meaning 'mind and heart,' and 开阔 (kāi kuò), meaning 'broad and open.' This idiom suggests someone who is open-minded, tolerant, and able to embrace different perspectives and situations. Although there is no specific historical story associated with this idiom, it is often used in literature to depict characters who are magnanimous and adaptable, such as in the works of the Qing dynasty author Gao Yang. In modern usage, it encourages individuals to be accepting and to maintain a positive outlook in relationships and challenges.

item.title: 落井下石 - Exploit misfortune
item.description: The idiom 落井下石 (luò jǐng xià shí) is commonly used in classical Chinese literature to describe a situation where someone takes advantage of another's misfortune to further harm them. The phrase literally means 'to throw stones (下石) at someone who has fallen into a well (落井).' This vivid imagery paints a picture of cruelty and opportunism, highlighting the despicable act of exacerbating someone's difficulties instead of offering help. In modern usage, this idiom serves as a moral reminder to show compassion and refrain from worsening others' hardships, emphasizing the importance of empathy and support in human relationships.

item.title: 恩将仇报 - Ingratitude and betrayal
item.description: The idiom 恩将仇报 (ēn jiāng chóu bào) is commonly used in classical Chinese literature to describe the act of repaying kindness with enmity. While its specific origin is not definitively tied to a single historical event, it is frequently illustrated through stories such as those found in the Ming dynasty's 'Journey to the West' (西游记) by Wu Cheng'en. In one notable episode, the character Sun Wukong (孙悟空) saves a princess, only to be falsely accused of wrongdoing, exemplifying the betrayal of good deeds. The idiom consists of four characters: 恩 (ēn, kindness), 将 (jiāng, to use), 仇 (chóu, enmity), and 报 (bào, to repay). In modern contexts, it is used to criticize acts of ingratitude and betrayal, highlighting the moral expectation to reciprocate kindness with gratitude rather than hostility.

item.title: 忘恩负义 - Ungrateful betrayal
item.description: Commonly used in classical Chinese, the idiom 忘恩负义 (wàng ēn fù yì) conveys the idea of forgetting kindness and betraying loyalty. The characters break down as follows: 忘 (wàng) means 'forget,' 恩 (ēn) refers to 'kindness' or 'favor,' 负 (fù) means 'betray,' and 义 (yì) stands for 'loyalty' or 'righteousness.' This idiom is often used to describe someone who is ungrateful and disloyal, turning against those who have helped them. In modern contexts, it serves as a cautionary term, reminding individuals of the importance of gratitude and loyalty in maintaining relationships.

item.title: 损人利己 - Selfish gain
item.description: The idiom 损人利己 (sǔn rén lì jǐ) is commonly used in classical Chinese literature to describe actions that benefit oneself at the expense of others. While there is no specific origin story tied to this idiom, it is often associated with the ethical and moral discussions found in ancient texts. The characters 损 (sǔn) mean 'harm' or 'damage,' 人 (rén) means 'people' or 'others,' 利 (lì) means 'benefit' or 'profit,' and 己 (jǐ) means 'oneself.' The idiom is used to criticize selfish behavior that disregards the well-being of others for personal gain. In modern contexts, it serves as a caution against opportunistic and unethical actions, emphasizing the importance of altruism and integrity in personal and professional relationships.

item.title: 以貌取人 - Superficial judgment
item.description: The idiom 以貌取人 (yǐ mào qǔ rén) originates from the historical text 《史记》 (Shǐjì) by Sima Qian, specifically from the chapter on Confucius' disciples. The story involves two of Confucius' students: Zai Yu, who was handsome, and Zi Yu, who was not. Confucius initially judged Zi Yu unfavorably due to his appearance, while he had high hopes for the eloquent Zai Yu. However, Zi Yu became a renowned scholar through diligence, while Zai Yu's laziness led to his downfall. This idiom literally means 'judge people by appearance' (以, by; 貌, appearance; 取, judge; 人, people) and serves as a caution against superficial judgments. In modern usage, it highlights the importance of looking beyond outward appearances to understand a person's true character.

item.title: 道貌岸然 - Hypocritical demeanor
item.description: The idiom 道貌岸然 (dào mào àn rán) is commonly used in classical Chinese literature to describe someone who appears solemn and dignified (道貌, dào mào) but is actually arrogant or hypocritical (岸然, àn rán). The imagery suggests a person with a serious demeanor, often used to criticize those who present themselves as morally upright while hiding less honorable intentions. This idiom is often employed in literature and conversation to highlight the contrast between outward appearances and inner character. In modern usage, it serves as a caution against judging people solely by their external behavior, reminding us to look beyond the facade.

item.title: 老当益壮 - Vigor despite age
item.description: The idiom 老当益壮 (lǎo dāng yì zhuàng) originates from the historical account of Ma Yuan, a renowned general during the Eastern Han Dynasty. As recorded in the 'Hou Han Shu' (后汉书), Ma Yuan, despite being in his sixties, demonstrated remarkable vigor and determination. When advised to retire, he famously declared, 'A man should become more resolute as he ages' (穷当益坚，老当益壮). The idiom breaks down as follows: 老 (lǎo) means 'old,' 当 (dāng) means 'should,' 益 (yì) means 'more,' and 壮 (zhuàng) means 'strong.' Today, this idiom is used to describe someone who, despite their age, remains energetic and spirited, often showing increased zeal and determination in their pursuits.

item.title: 童叟无欺 - Honest business practices
item.description: The idiom 童叟无欺 (tóng sǒu wú qī) originates from the late Qing dynasty, specifically from the novel 《二十年目睹之怪现状》 by Wu Jianren. It describes a business practice of honesty and fairness, ensuring that neither children (童, tóng) nor elderly (叟, sǒu) are deceived (无欺, wú qī). This idiom emphasizes the importance of integrity in commerce, advocating for fair treatment of all customers regardless of age. In modern usage, it is often seen in business contexts to highlight a commitment to ethical practices and trustworthy transactions.

item.title: 尊老爱幼 - Intergenerational harmony
item.description: The idiom 尊老爱幼 (zūn lǎo ài yòu) is commonly used in classical Chinese literature to emphasize the moral virtues of respecting the elderly (尊, respect) and caring for the young (爱, love). While the exact origin of this phrase is not tied to a specific historical event, it embodies a long-standing cultural value in Chinese society. This concept is deeply rooted in Confucian teachings, which advocate for harmonious family relationships and social stability. The idiom paints a vivid picture of a society where elders are honored and children are nurtured, reflecting the ideal of a caring and respectful community. In modern usage, it serves as a reminder of the importance of maintaining these virtues in everyday life, promoting intergenerational harmony and moral integrity.

item.title: 人尽其才 - Maximize individual potential
item.description: The idiom 人尽其才 (rén jìn qí cái) is commonly used in classical Chinese literature to express the idea of utilizing everyone's talents to their fullest potential. The phrase is derived from the work 《淮南子》 by Liu An during the Han dynasty, which discusses the importance of employing people according to their abilities. The idiom is illustrated by a story about the philosopher Gongsun Long, who accepted a disciple with an extraordinary loud voice. Initially ridiculed by others, the disciple's unique skill proved invaluable when he successfully summoned a distant boatman to assist their group. The characters 人 (rén) mean 'people,' 尽 (jìn) means 'to exhaust' or 'to fully use,' 其 (qí) means 'their,' and 才 (cái) means 'talent.' In modern usage, this idiom emphasizes the importance of recognizing and placing individuals in roles where they can best contribute, ensuring that everyone's abilities are effectively utilized.

item.title: 物尽其用 - Maximize resource use
item.description: Commonly used in classical Chinese, the idiom 物尽其用 (wù jìn qí yòng) emphasizes the principle of maximizing the utility of resources. Although its earliest specific origin is not well-documented, the concept is reflected in various classical texts, such as the idea presented in 《长短经》 (Cháng Duǎn Jīng), which discusses the importance of using things to their full potential. The idiom breaks down into 物 (wù, thing), 尽 (jìn, exhaust), 其 (qí, its), and 用 (yòng, use), painting a vivid picture of ensuring that every resource is fully utilized. In modern contexts, it encourages efficiency and resourcefulness, urging individuals and organizations to make the most of what they have at their disposal.

item.title: 众望所归 - Trusted leadership
item.description: The idiom 众望所归 (zhòng wàng suǒ guī) is commonly used in classical Chinese literature to describe a person who has earned the trust and admiration of the public due to their high reputation and moral standing. The phrase first appeared in the historical text 《晋书·列传三十传论》, where it described a figure named 建兴, who was the focus of public expectation and trust. The characters 众 (zhòng) mean 'the masses' or 'the public,' 望 (wàng) refers to 'hope' or 'expectation,' 所 (suǒ) means 'that which,' and 归 (guī) means 'to return' or 'to belong.' In modern usage, this idiom is often applied to individuals who are chosen for leadership roles or honors because they are widely respected and trusted by the community.

item.title: 有口皆碑 - Universally praised
item.description: The idiom 有口皆碑 (yǒu kǒu jiē bēi) is rooted in classical Chinese literature, with its earliest known mention in the Song dynasty text 《五灯会元》 by Shi Puji. The phrase '劝君不用镌顽石，路上行人口似碑' suggests that the spoken praise of people (口, mouth) is akin to a monument (碑, stone tablet) that commemorates one's virtues. This idea was later popularized in the Qing dynasty novel 《老残游记》 by Liu E, where a character praises an official's achievements as being universally recognized and celebrated. The idiom vividly illustrates how genuine admiration and commendation from others can serve as a lasting testament to one's character and deeds, much like an inscribed stone tablet. In modern usage, it describes someone who is widely praised and respected.

item.title: 先发制人 - Seize initiative
item.description: The idiom 先发制人 (xiān fā zhì rén) originates from the historical context of the late Qin Dynasty, as recorded in the 'Records of the Grand Historian' (史记) by Sima Qian. In 209 BCE, during the uprising against the Qin Dynasty, Xiang Liang and his nephew Xiang Yu were strategizing their next move. Xiang Liang advised that by taking the initiative (先发, xiān fā), they could control (制, zhì) their opponents (人, rén). This strategic insight led them to act decisively, ultimately contributing to their temporary success against the Qin forces. The idiom emphasizes the importance of taking the initiative to gain control over a situation. In modern usage, it is applied in various contexts such as business, politics, and personal decision-making, where acting first can provide a strategic advantage.

item.title: 后发制人 - Strategic patience
item.description: The idiom 后发制人 (hòu fā zhì rén) originates from the Warring States period text 《荀子·议兵》, attributed to the philosopher Xunzi and his disciples. The phrase means 'to act later to control others' and is rooted in military strategy. The historical context involves the story of the Spring and Autumn period, where the exiled prince Chong'er of Jin, later known as Duke Wen of Jin (晋文公), returned to power with strategic patience. During a conflict with the state of Chu, he initially retreated to avoid direct confrontation, allowing the enemy to become overconfident. This strategic withdrawal (后, later) enabled him to launch a decisive counterattack (制, control; 人, others) when conditions were favorable. In modern usage, this idiom describes a strategic approach where one waits for the opportune moment to act, ensuring a tactical advantage.

item.title: 兵不厌诈 - Strategic deception
item.description: The idiom 兵不厌诈 (bīng bù yàn zhà) originates from the Warring States period, specifically from the text 《韩非子》 (Hán Fēi Zǐ). It recounts a story involving the state of Jin and its ruler, Duke Wen (晋文公), during a conflict with the state of Chu. To honor a past promise, Duke Wen initially retreated, luring the Chu forces into a trap. His advisor suggested using deception (诈, zhà) to outsmart the enemy, leading to a strategic victory at the Battle of Chengpu. The idiom emphasizes the use of cunning and deception in warfare, suggesting that in battle, all tactics, including deceit, are permissible. In modern contexts, it is used to describe situations where strategic deception is employed to achieve success.

item.title: 知己知彼 - Strategic insight
item.description: The idiom 知己知彼 (zhī jǐ zhī bǐ) originates from the classic military treatise 《孙子兵法》 (The Art of War) by Sun Tzu, a renowned military strategist from the Spring and Autumn period. In the chapter titled 《谋攻》 (Móu Gōng), Sun Tzu emphasizes that understanding both oneself (知己, zhī jǐ) and the enemy (知彼, zhī bǐ) is crucial for achieving success in battle. This principle was famously applied by Sun Tzu in advising King Helü of Wu during his campaign against the state of Chu. By thoroughly analyzing both his own strengths and the weaknesses of the enemy, Sun Tzu helped the Wu army achieve a decisive victory. In modern usage, this idiom underscores the importance of comprehensive knowledge and preparation in various fields, from business to diplomacy.

item.title: 出奇制胜 - Unexpected strategy
item.description: The idiom 出奇制胜 (chū qí zhì shèng) originates from the ancient military text 《孙子兵法》 (The Art of War) by Sun Tzu during the Spring and Autumn period. It describes a strategic approach where unexpected tactics (出奇, chū qí) are used to achieve victory (制胜, zhì shèng). A famous historical example is the story of Tian Dan, a military strategist from the Qi state, who used unconventional methods to defeat the Yan army during the Warring States period. By employing creative tactics, such as using fire-carrying cattle to cause chaos, Tian Dan was able to reclaim lost territories. In modern usage, this idiom is employed to describe situations where innovative and surprising strategies lead to success.

item.title: 避实就虚 - Strategic evasion
item.description: The idiom 避实就虚 (bì shí jiù xū) originates from the ancient Chinese military text, 《孙子兵法》 (The Art of War) by Sun Tzu, specifically from the chapter on '虚实' (Xū Shí, 'Weak and Strong Points'). Sun Tzu, a renowned military strategist from the Spring and Autumn period, advised that in warfare, one should avoid (避, bì) the enemy's strong points (实, shí) and attack (就, jiù) their weak points (虚, xū). This strategic principle emphasizes flexibility and adaptability, akin to water flowing around obstacles. In modern usage, the idiom extends beyond military contexts to describe avoiding substantial issues and focusing on less critical matters, often implying evasiveness or strategic maneuvering.

item.title: 调虎离山 - Divert and conquer
item.description: The idiom 调虎离山 (diào hǔ lí shān) originates from the classic military text 《三十六计》 (Thirty-Six Stratagems), specifically the strategy of luring the tiger away from its mountain. This tactic was famously employed by Sun Ce during the late Eastern Han Dynasty. Sun Ce, facing a strong adversary, Liu Xun, devised a plan to lure Liu Xun away from his fortified position by appealing to his greed. Sun Ce sent gifts and flattery, convincing Liu Xun to attack another target, leaving his base vulnerable. Sun Ce then swiftly captured Liu Xun's territory. The characters 调 (diào, lure), 虎 (hǔ, tiger), 离 (lí, leave), and 山 (shān, mountain) vividly illustrate the strategy of drawing a powerful opponent away from their stronghold. In modern usage, it describes using cunning to weaken a formidable adversary by diverting their attention.

item.title: 打草惊蛇 - Alert the enemy
item.description: The idiom 打草惊蛇 (dǎ cǎo jīng shé) originates from a story during the Southern Tang dynasty. A county magistrate named Wang Lu was known for his corrupt practices. When a group of citizens attempted to report the misconduct of his subordinate, Wang Lu realized that their actions could expose his own wrongdoings. He remarked, '汝虽打草，吾已蛇惊,' meaning 'Though you hit the grass, I am already alarmed like a snake.' The idiom (打, hit) (草, grass) (惊, startle) (蛇, snake) illustrates the idea of inadvertently alerting an adversary through careless actions. In modern usage, it serves as a caution against actions that might prematurely alert others, often used in strategic contexts to emphasize the importance of discretion.

item.title: 借刀杀人 - Indirect manipulation
item.description: The idiom 借刀杀人 (jiè dāo shā rén) is a strategic concept from the ancient Chinese military text 'The Thirty-Six Stratagems' (三十六计), specifically the third stratagem. It translates to 'Kill with a borrowed knife,' illustrating a tactic where one uses another's resources or power to achieve one's own objectives, often without direct involvement. The characters break down as follows: 借 (jiè) means 'borrow,' 刀 (dāo) means 'knife,' 杀 (shā) means 'kill,' and 人 (rén) means 'person.' This strategy is about manipulating others into doing one's dirty work, thereby avoiding direct confrontation or blame. In modern contexts, it describes situations where individuals or groups cleverly maneuver others to act on their behalf, often in business or political scenarios.

item.title: 趁火打劫 - Exploit vulnerability
item.description: The idiom 趁火打劫 (chèn huǒ dǎ jié) originates from the ancient Chinese military text 《三十六计》 (Thirty-Six Stratagems), specifically the fifth stratagem. It describes the tactic of taking advantage of an enemy's misfortune or chaos to gain an advantage. The idiom is vividly illustrated in the story of the Spring and Autumn period, where the state of Yue, under King Goujian, exploited the weakened state of Wu to reclaim power. The characters break down as follows: 趁 (chèn, take advantage of), 火 (huǒ, fire), 打 (dǎ, hit), and 劫 (jié, rob). In modern usage, it refers to opportunistically exploiting someone's vulnerability or misfortune for personal gain, often with a negative connotation.

item.title: 金蝉脱壳 - Strategic escape
item.description: The idiom 金蝉脱壳 (jīn chán tuō qiào) is commonly used in classical Chinese literature and does not have a specific origin story tied to a historical text. The phrase literally translates to 'the golden cicada sheds its shell.' It vividly paints the image of a cicada leaving behind its old shell as it emerges anew. This imagery is often used to describe a strategic retreat or escape, where one leaves behind something to divert attention or create an illusion, allowing for a successful evasion or transformation. In modern contexts, it is used to describe situations where someone cleverly extricates themselves from a difficult situation, often leaving behind a misleading impression or decoy.

item.title: 顺手牵羊 - Exploit small opportunities
item.description: The idiom 顺手牵羊 (shùn shǒu qiān yáng) originates from the ancient Chinese military text 《三十六计》 (Thirty-Six Stratagems), specifically the strategy of exploiting small opportunities for gain. The phrase literally means 'to lead away a sheep in passing,' illustrating the tactic of seizing minor advantages when they present themselves. The historical context involves the Spring and Autumn period, where the Qi state minister, 崔杼 (Cui Zhu), cleverly eliminated his rival, 齐庄公 (Duke Zhuang of Qi), by capitalizing on a moment of vulnerability. The characters 顺 (shùn) mean 'smoothly' or 'conveniently,' 手 (shǒu) means 'hand,' 牵 (qiān) means 'to lead,' and 羊 (yáng) means 'sheep.' In modern usage, this idiom often carries a negative connotation, referring to taking advantage of a situation to benefit oneself, sometimes unscrupulously.

item.title: 瞒天过海 - Disguise true intentions
item.description: The idiom 瞒天过海 (mán tiān guò hǎi) originates from the first stratagem of the 'Thirty-Six Stratagems' (三十六计). The story is linked to the Tang Dynasty, where the general Xue Rengui (薛仁贵) helped Emperor Taizong (唐太宗) cross the sea without the emperor realizing it. This was achieved by constructing a 'wooden city' to disguise the crossing, thus calming the emperor's fear of the sea. The characters 瞒 (mán) mean 'to deceive' and 天 (tiān) means 'heaven,' while 过 (guò) means 'to cross' and 海 (hǎi) means 'sea.' The tactic involves using deception to hide true intentions, making the enemy complacent. In modern usage, it describes strategies where one uses common actions to disguise a significant move, often applied in military, political, and business contexts.

item.title: 李代桃僵 - Substitute or take the fall
item.description: The idiom 李代桃僵 (lǐ dài táo jiāng) originates from a poem in the 乐府诗集 (Yuèfǔ Shījí), which describes the rise and fall of a family during the Han dynasty. The poem tells of five brothers who, after gaining favor with the emperor, live in luxury but eventually face downfall due to their misdeeds. When one brother is punished, the others fail to support him, highlighting their lack of solidarity. The idiom itself paints a vivid image of a plum tree (李, lǐ) dying in place of a peach tree (桃, táo) when its roots are eaten by insects, symbolizing sacrifice and substitution. In modern usage, 李代桃僵 is used to describe situations where one person takes the fall or substitutes for another, often in the context of bearing consequences for someone else's actions.

item.title: 反客为主 - Seize control
item.description: The idiom 反客为主 (fǎn kè wéi zhǔ) originates from the historical context of the Three Kingdoms period, particularly involving the strategic maneuvers of Liu Bei's forces against the formidable general Xiahou Yuan. As Liu Bei advanced on Hanzhong, his advisor Fa Zheng suggested a strategy to gradually turn the tables on the defending forces, effectively transforming the 'guest' (客, kè) into the 'host' (主, zhǔ). This involved careful planning and seizing the right moment to strike, ultimately leading to a decisive victory. The idiom illustrates the concept of reversing roles and taking control of a situation. In modern usage, it describes the act of turning a disadvantageous position into an advantageous one by seizing opportunities and acting decisively.

item.title: 擒贼擒王 - Target the leader
item.description: The idiom 擒贼擒王 (qín zéi qín wáng) originates from a line in the Tang dynasty poet Du Fu's work, which states, '射人先射马，擒贼先擒王' (shè rén xiān shè mǎ, qín zéi xiān qín wáng), meaning 'shoot the horse before the man, capture the king before the thief.' This line was later encapsulated into the idiom. The historical context involves the An Lushan Rebellion during the Tang dynasty, where the general Zhang Xun aimed to capture the enemy leader Yinzhiqi to turn the tide of battle. The idiom breaks down to 擒 (qín, capture), 贼 (zéi, thief), 擒 (qín, capture), 王 (wáng, king). In modern usage, it suggests that to solve a problem effectively, one should target the root cause or the main figure, akin to the saying 'cut off the head and the body will fall.'

item.title: 关门捉贼 - Trap and eliminate
item.description: The idiom 关门捉贼 (guān mén zhuō zéi) is derived from the ancient Chinese military strategy found in the 'Thirty-Six Stratagems' (三十六计), specifically the 27th stratagem. It involves the tactic of 'shutting the door to catch the thief,' which means to trap and eliminate an enemy by cutting off their escape routes. Historically, this strategy has been employed in various military contexts, such as the famous Battle of Changping during the Warring States period, where the Qin general Bai Qi used it to encircle and defeat the Zhao army. The characters 关 (guān) mean 'close' or 'shut,' 门 (mén) means 'door,' 捉 (zhuō) means 'catch,' and 贼 (zéi) means 'thief.' In modern usage, this idiom is applied to situations where one strategically limits options to ensure the capture or defeat of an opponent.

item.title: 远交近攻 - Strategic prioritization
item.description: The idiom 远交近攻 (yuǎn jiāo jìn gōng) originates from the strategic counsel given by the advisor Fan Ju to King Zhaoxiang of Qin during the Warring States period, as recorded in the 'Strategies of the Warring States' (《战国策》). Fan Ju advised the king to 'befriend distant states and attack nearby ones' to expand Qin's territory effectively. By forming alliances with distant states (远, distant; 交, ally) and focusing military efforts on neighboring adversaries (近, near; 攻, attack), Qin could avoid overextending its resources and gradually increase its power. This strategy laid the groundwork for Qin's eventual unification of China. In modern contexts, the idiom suggests a methodical approach to problem-solving by addressing immediate challenges while maintaining broader alliances.

item.title: 假道伐虢 - Exploitation through deception
item.description: This strategic idiom originates from the historical account in the 'Zuo Zhuan' (左传), a classical Chinese text. During the Spring and Autumn period, the state of Jin (晋国) sought to conquer the neighboring state of Guo (虢国). To achieve this, Jin needed to pass through the state of Yu (虞国). The Jin minister, Xun Xi (荀息), advised offering Yu valuable gifts to borrow their territory as a pathway. Despite warnings from his advisor Gong Zhiqi (宫之奇), the ruler of Yu accepted the gifts and allowed Jin's army to pass. Jin subsequently attacked and conquered Guo, and later turned on Yu, annexing it as well. The idiom '假道伐虢' (jiǎ dào fá guó) literally means 'borrow the way to attack Guo' and highlights the danger of short-sighted decisions. In modern usage, it warns against allowing others to exploit one's resources for their own gain.

item.title: 树上开花 - Create favorable illusion
item.description: Commonly used in classical Chinese, the idiom 树上开花 (shù shàng kāi huā) is part of the Thirty-Six Stratagems, specifically the tenth strategy. It involves creating a favorable situation through deception or illusion, akin to making flowers bloom on a tree. A historical example is the story of Zhang Fei during the Three Kingdoms period, who used a dust-raising tactic to intimidate Cao Cao's forces. The characters 树 (shù, tree), 上 (shàng, on), 开 (kāi, bloom), and 花 (huā, flower) evoke the image of something unexpected and impressive. In modern contexts, it symbolizes using clever strategies to create advantageous conditions, often in business or negotiations, by making something appear more valuable or formidable than it is.

item.title: 空城计 - Strategic bluff
item.description: The idiom 空城计 (kōng chéng jì) originates from the classic Chinese novel 《三国演义》 (Romance of the Three Kingdoms) by 罗贯中 (Luo Guanzhong). The story takes place during the Three Kingdoms period, specifically in 228 CE. The brilliant strategist 诸葛亮 (Zhuge Liang) found himself in a precarious situation when the formidable Wei general 司马懿 (Sima Yi) advanced with a large army towards the city he was defending. With limited troops (only 2500 soldiers), Zhuge Liang devised a daring plan: he ordered the city gates to be opened and instructed his men to disguise themselves as civilians cleaning the streets. Meanwhile, he sat atop the city wall, calmly playing the zither (琴, qín). This unexpected display of confidence led Sima Yi to suspect an ambush, prompting him to retreat. The idiom 空城计 (空, empty; 城, city; 计, strategy) symbolizes a strategic bluff, using psychological tactics to deter an opponent. In modern usage, it refers to disguising one's weaknesses to mislead others.

item.title: 苦肉计 - Self-sacrifice strategy
item.description: The idiom 苦肉计 (kǔ ròu jì) originates from the classic Chinese novel 《三国演义》 (Romance of the Three Kingdoms) by Luo Guanzhong. It tells the story of a clever strategy devised by Zhou Yu and Huang Gai during the Battle of Red Cliffs. To deceive the warlord Cao Cao, Huang Gai volunteered to endure a severe beating (苦, suffering; 肉, flesh) to feign defection. This act of self-sacrifice convinced Cao Cao of Huang Gai's sincerity, allowing the allied forces of Sun Quan and Liu Bei to execute a successful fire attack on Cao Cao's fleet. The idiom illustrates a tactic where one endures self-inflicted harm to gain an opponent's trust. In modern usage, 苦肉计 is employed to describe situations where individuals endure hardship to achieve a greater strategic goal.

item.title: 连环计 - Complex strategic plan
item.description: The idiom 连环计 (lián huán jì) originates from the classic Chinese military text 《三十六计》 (Thirty-Six Stratagems), specifically the thirty-fifth stratagem. This strategy involves using a series of interconnected tactics (连, interlock; 环, ring; 计, stratagem) to weaken an opponent. A famous historical example is the Battle of Red Cliffs during the Three Kingdoms period, where the strategist Zhou Yu employed multiple strategies in succession, such as the use of deception and fire attacks, to defeat the numerically superior forces of Cao Cao. The idiom emphasizes the importance of using a combination of strategies that are linked together to create a compounded effect, making it difficult for the enemy to respond effectively. In modern contexts, it describes a situation where multiple strategies are used in conjunction to achieve a complex goal.

item.title: 走为上计 - Strategic retreat
item.description: The idiom 走为上计 (zǒu wéi shàng jì) originates from a historical event during the Southern and Northern Dynasties. It is associated with the rebellion led by Wang Jingze against Xiao Luan, the future Emperor Ming of Qi. Faced with overwhelming odds, Xiao Luan and his son chose to retreat, embodying the strategy of 'retreat as the best plan.' This idiom is often linked to the ancient military tactics known as the 'Thirty-Six Stratagems,' specifically highlighting the wisdom of strategic withdrawal to preserve strength for future battles. In modern usage, it suggests that sometimes the best course of action in difficult situations is to temporarily withdraw or avoid confrontation to regroup and reassess.

item.title: 一网打尽 - Comprehensive approach
item.description: Commonly used in classical Chinese, the idiom 一网打尽 (yī wǎng dǎ jìn) paints a vivid image of capturing everything in one net. The phrase is often used to describe a strategy or action that aims to achieve total success or to leave nothing behind. The characters 一 (yī) mean 'one', 网 (wǎng) means 'net', 打 (dǎ) means 'to hit or strike', and 尽 (jìn) means 'to exhaust or use up'. In modern usage, this idiom is applied in contexts where a comprehensive or all-encompassing approach is taken, such as in business strategies or problem-solving scenarios, where the goal is to address all aspects or capture all opportunities in one decisive action.

item.title: 围而不攻 - Strategic patience
item.description: Commonly used in classical Chinese, the idiom 围而不攻 (wéi ér bù gōng) paints a vivid picture of a strategic military tactic. The characters 围 (wéi) mean 'to surround,' while 不攻 (bù gōng) mean 'not to attack.' This idiom describes a situation where an army surrounds an enemy but deliberately chooses not to attack. This tactic can be used to cut off supplies and force a surrender without direct confrontation. In modern contexts, it suggests a strategy of exerting pressure without immediate action, often to gain a psychological advantage or to wait for a more opportune moment.

item.title: 避其锐气 - Strategic patience
item.description: This strategic idiom originates from the ancient military text 'The Art of War' by Sun Tzu (孙子兵法). It advises military leaders to avoid (避, bì) confronting the enemy's initial vigor (锐气, ruì qì) when their morale is high. Instead, it suggests waiting until the enemy becomes fatigued and complacent before launching an attack. This tactic was famously applied during the Battle of Changshao (长勺之战) in the Spring and Autumn period, where the strategist Cao Gui (曹刿) led the state of Lu to victory by exploiting the enemy's diminishing morale. In modern contexts, this idiom is used to describe strategies in competitive fields, emphasizing the importance of timing and patience in overcoming opponents.

item.title: 朝三暮四 - Fickle and inconsistent
item.description: This idiom, 朝三暮四 (zhāo sān mù sì), originates from the classical text 'Zhuangzi' (庄子), specifically from the chapter 'Qi Wu Lun' (齐物论). The story involves an old man known as 'Juguong' (狙公), who kept many monkeys. Due to limited resources, he decided to ration their food. Initially, he proposed giving them three acorns in the morning (朝, morning) and four in the evening (暮, evening), which angered the monkeys. However, when he reversed the order to four in the morning and three in the evening, the monkeys were satisfied, despite the total being unchanged. This idiom illustrates the folly of being swayed by superficial changes without understanding the underlying reality. In modern usage, it describes someone who is fickle or easily deceived by appearances.

item.title: 唇齿相依 - Mutual reliance
item.description: The idiom 唇齿相依 (chún chǐ xiāng yī) originates from the historical context of the Spring and Autumn period, as recorded in the ancient text 《左传》 (Zuǒ Zhuàn). The story involves the state of Jin seeking passage through the state of Yu to attack the state of Guo. A wise advisor, Gong Zhiqi, warned that if Guo fell, Yu would be next, as they were closely linked like lips and teeth. This idiom was later used during the Three Kingdoms period when the states of Wu and Shu formed a strategic alliance, described as being as interdependent as lips and teeth. The characters 唇 (chún, lips) and 齿 (chǐ, teeth) symbolize mutual reliance. In modern usage, it describes relationships or situations where parties are closely connected and dependent on each other.

item.title: 沉默寡言 - Reserved and thoughtful
item.description: The idiom 沉默寡言 (chén mò guǎ yán) is commonly used in classical Chinese literature to describe a person who is quiet and speaks very little. It is often associated with individuals who possess a calm and reserved demeanor, preferring to listen rather than speak. The idiom is composed of four characters: 沉 (chén), meaning 'silent'; 默 (mò), meaning 'quiet'; 寡 (guǎ), meaning 'few'; and 言 (yán), meaning 'words'. Together, they paint a vivid picture of someone who is not inclined to engage in much conversation. In modern usage, this idiom is used to describe people who are introverted or thoughtful, often implying a sense of wisdom or depth beneath their quiet exterior.

item.title: 大义灭亲 - Justice over family
item.description: The idiom 大义灭亲 (dà yì miè qīn) originates from the historical text 《左传》 (Zuo Zhuan), specifically from the story of Shi Que (石碏) during the Spring and Autumn period. Shi Que was a minister in the state of Wei (卫), and his son Shi Hou (石厚) conspired with Zhou Xu (州吁) to murder the ruler, Huan Gong (桓公), and seize power. Despite his familial ties, Shi Que prioritized justice and righteousness (大义, great justice) over personal relationships (亲, kin). He reported their crimes to the neighboring state of Chen, leading to their capture and execution. This idiom illustrates the moral courage to uphold justice even at the cost of family bonds. In modern usage, it describes the act of sacrificing personal relationships to uphold justice and integrity.

item.title: 独善其身 - Self-focus in hardship
item.description: The idiom 独善其身 (dú shàn qí shēn) originates from the teachings of Mencius (孟子), a prominent Confucian philosopher from the Warring States period. In the text 《孟子·尽心上》, Mencius advises that when one is in a difficult or unrewarding situation (穷, qióng), it is important to focus on self-cultivation and maintain personal integrity (独善其身). Conversely, when one achieves success or influence (达, dá), they should strive to benefit the wider world (兼济天下). The idiom emphasizes the importance of self-improvement and moral integrity, especially when external circumstances are unfavorable. In modern usage, it often carries a slightly negative connotation, suggesting someone who is overly focused on their own well-being without regard for others.

item.title: 返老还童 - Regain vitality
item.description: The idiom 返老还童 (fǎn lǎo huán tóng) is commonly used in classical Chinese to describe the concept of reversing aging and returning to a youthful state. While it does not have a specific origin story in ancient texts, it vividly paints the image of someone becoming young again. The characters 返 (fǎn) mean 'return,' 老 (lǎo) mean 'old,' 还 (huán) mean 'return,' and 童 (tóng) mean 'child.' In modern usage, this idiom is often used metaphorically to describe someone who has regained vitality or youthful energy, as if they have turned back the clock on aging.

item.title: 防患未然 - Prepare in advance
item.description: The idiom 防患未然 (fáng huàn wèi rán) is commonly used in classical Chinese literature to emphasize the importance of taking preventive measures before problems arise. The characters 防 (fáng) mean 'to prevent,' 患 (huàn) refers to 'troubles' or 'disasters,' 未 (wèi) means 'not yet,' and 然 (rán) signifies 'happened.' Together, they paint a vivid image of guarding against potential issues before they manifest. Although there is no specific historical story linked to this idiom, it embodies the wisdom of foresight and preparedness. In modern contexts, it is often used to advise individuals and organizations to plan ahead and mitigate risks proactively.

item.title: 丰功伟绩 - Outstanding contributions
item.description: The idiom 丰功伟绩 (fēng gōng wěi jì) is commonly used in classical Chinese to describe significant achievements and contributions to society. While the exact origin is not tied to a specific historical story, it is mentioned in the works of Song dynasty official Bao Zheng (宋·包拯) in 《天章阁对策》. The characters 丰 (fēng) mean 'abundant' or 'grand', 功 (gōng) refers to 'merit' or 'achievement', 伟 (wěi) signifies 'great' or 'extraordinary', and 绩 (jì) originally comes from a weaving term meaning 'to accumulate'. Together, they form a powerful expression highlighting the monumental accomplishments that result from sustained effort and perseverance. In modern usage, it is often used to commend individuals or groups who have made outstanding contributions, especially in historical or societal contexts.

item.title: 风调雨顺 - Peace and prosperity
item.description: The idiom 风调雨顺 (fēng tiáo yǔ shùn) is commonly used in classical Chinese to describe favorable weather conditions essential for agriculture. The phrase originates from ancient texts, such as the 《旧唐书》 (Old Book of Tang), which quotes the 《六韬》 (Six Secret Teachings), illustrating how harmonious winds (风, wind) and timely rains (雨, rain) lead to bountiful harvests. This idiom reflects the deep connection between agricultural success and natural elements, emphasizing the importance of balanced weather for prosperity. In modern usage, it extends beyond agriculture to symbolize peace and stability in society, often paired with '国泰民安' (guó tài mín ān), meaning 'the country prospers and the people live in peace.'

item.title: 改过自新 - Self-improvement
item.description: Commonly used in classical Chinese, the idiom 改过自新 (gǎi guò zì xīn) conveys the idea of self-improvement and personal transformation. The characters 改 (gǎi) mean 'change' or 'correct,' 过 (guò) means 'mistake,' 自 (zì) means 'oneself,' and 新 (xīn) means 'new.' This idiom paints a vivid picture of someone acknowledging their past errors and making a conscious effort to renew themselves. In modern usage, it encourages individuals to reflect on their past actions, learn from their mistakes, and strive for personal growth and renewal.

item.title: 高瞻远瞩 - Foresight and vision
item.description: The idiom 高瞻远瞩 (gāo zhān yuǎn zhǔ) is commonly used in classical Chinese literature to describe someone with great foresight and vision. The phrase is composed of four characters: 高 (gāo, high), 瞻 (zhān, to look), 远 (yuǎn, far), and 瞩 (zhǔ, to gaze). Together, they paint a vivid image of someone standing at a high vantage point, looking far into the distance. This idiom is often used to praise individuals who can see beyond immediate concerns and anticipate future developments. It suggests wisdom and strategic thinking, akin to having a bird's-eye view of a situation. In modern usage, it encourages people to adopt a long-term perspective and consider the broader implications of their actions.

item.title: 各行其是 - Disunity in action
item.description: The idiom 各行其是 (gè xíng qí shì) is commonly used in classical Chinese literature to describe a situation where individuals act according to their own beliefs or opinions, without a unified direction. The phrase is often associated with the idea of people following their own paths, leading to a lack of cohesion. The characters 各 (gè) mean 'each,' 行 (xíng) means 'to act,' 其 (qí) means 'their,' and 是 (shì) means 'right.' In modern usage, this idiom is often used to criticize situations where there is a lack of coordination or agreement, leading to inefficiency or conflict. It serves as a reminder of the importance of unity and collaboration in achieving common goals.

item.title: 公而忘私 - Selfless dedication
item.description: The idiom 公而忘私 (gōng ér wàng sī) originates from the Han Dynasty, specifically from the writings of Ban Gu in the 'Book of Han' (汉书). It tells the story of Jia Yi, a talented young scholar who was recognized for his wisdom and integrity. Despite facing jealousy and false accusations from others, Jia Yi advised Emperor Wen of Han to prioritize public welfare over personal interests. The characters 公 (gōng) and 私 (sī) mean 'public' and 'private,' respectively, while 忘 (wàng) means 'to forget.' Thus, the idiom literally means 'publicly forget the private.' In modern usage, it describes the virtue of selflessly prioritizing the collective good over personal gain, a quality admired in leaders and citizens alike.

item.title: 孤注一掷 - All-in risk
item.description: The idiom 孤注一掷 (gū zhù yī zhì) is commonly used in classical Chinese literature to describe a situation where one stakes everything on a single venture. The phrase paints a vivid image of placing one's solitary bet (孤注) in a gamble, emphasizing the act of making a decisive and risky move (一掷). While the specific historical origin of this idiom is not clearly documented, it is often associated with strategic decisions in warfare or high-stakes situations where success hinges on a single, bold action. In modern contexts, it is used to describe scenarios where individuals or groups take significant risks, often with the hope of achieving great rewards, but with the understanding that failure could lead to substantial losses.

item.title: 顾此失彼 - Neglect due to distraction
item.description: The idiom 顾此失彼 (gù cǐ shī bǐ) originates from the Ming dynasty text 《东周列国志》 by Feng Menglong. It describes a military strategy by Wu Zixu, who divided his army into three groups to attack different targets. The phrase captures the challenge of focusing on one aspect (顾此, attend to this) while neglecting another (失彼, lose that), illustrating the pitfalls of spreading resources too thin. In modern contexts, it is used to describe situations where limited resources lead to oversight or neglect, such as in project management or public policy. This idiom serves as a cautionary reminder to balance attention and resources effectively.

item.title: 含辛茹苦 - Persevere through adversity
item.description: Commonly used in classical Chinese literature, the idiom 含辛茹苦 (hán xīn rú kǔ) vividly portrays the enduring of hardships and suffering. The phrase is composed of two parts: 含辛 (hán xīn), where 辛 (xīn) refers to the bitterness or spiciness one endures, and 茹苦 (rú kǔ), with 苦 (kǔ) symbolizing the act of swallowing or enduring bitterness. This idiom is often associated with the perseverance required to achieve success, reflecting the idea that enduring hardships is a necessary part of the journey toward enlightenment or accomplishment. In modern usage, 含辛茹苦 is employed to describe individuals who have gone through significant struggles, often highlighting the sacrifices made by parents or pioneers to provide better opportunities for future generations.

item.title: 好高骛远 - Unrealistic aspirations
item.description: The idiom 好高骛远 (hào gāo wù yuǎn) is commonly used in classical Chinese literature to describe someone who sets overly ambitious goals without considering their current capabilities. The characters paint a vivid image: 好 (hào) means 'to like' or 'to be fond of,' 高 (gāo) means 'high,' 骛 (wù) means 'to pursue,' and 远 (yuǎn) means 'far.' Together, they depict a person who is fond of pursuing high and distant goals. In modern usage, this idiom serves as a caution against unrealistic aspirations and encourages individuals to set achievable objectives based on their present situation.

item.title: 鸿鹄之志 - Great aspirations
item.description: The idiom 鸿鹄之志 (hóng hú zhī zhì) originates from the story of Chen Sheng (陈胜), a figure from the late Qin Dynasty. As a young man, Chen Sheng worked as a farmhand. One day, while resting from labor, he expressed his ambition to achieve wealth and status, saying, 'If I become rich and noble, I will not forget my friends.' His peers laughed, doubting his lofty aspirations. Chen Sheng responded with, 'How can sparrows understand the ambitions of swans?' (燕雀安知鸿鹄之志哉). This phrase highlights the contrast between those with grand ambitions (鸿鹄, swans) and those with limited vision (燕雀, sparrows). The idiom symbolizes having great aspirations and the perseverance to achieve them, despite humble beginnings or skepticism from others.

item.title: 花好月圆 - Perfect happiness
item.description: The idiom 花好月圆 (huā hǎo yuè yuán) is commonly used in classical Chinese to describe a state of perfect happiness and harmony, often in the context of relationships or family gatherings. The characters themselves paint a vivid picture: 花 (huā) means 'flower,' 好 (hǎo) means 'good,' 月 (yuè) means 'moon,' and 圆 (yuán) means 'round.' Together, they evoke the image of a beautiful night with blooming flowers and a full moon, symbolizing completeness and joy. This idiom is frequently used to express wishes for a harmonious and fulfilling relationship, especially during weddings or festive occasions. It captures the essence of an ideal moment where everything is in its perfect place, reflecting both aesthetic beauty and emotional fulfillment.

item.title: 患难与共 - Solidarity in hardship
item.description: The idiom 患难与共 (huàn nàn yǔ gòng) is commonly used in classical Chinese literature to describe the act of sharing burdens and facing difficulties together. It is believed to have its roots in the Confucian classic 'Liji' (礼记), specifically in the 'Ruxing' (儒行) chapter, which emphasizes the ethical principle of mutual support during times of hardship. The idiom is composed of four characters: 患 (huàn, trouble), 难 (nàn, difficulty), 与 (yǔ, with), and 共 (gòng, together). This phrase vividly conveys the idea of solidarity and collective resilience. In modern usage, it is often applied to describe relationships where individuals support each other through challenging circumstances, such as in marriages, friendships, or business partnerships, highlighting the importance of loyalty and cooperation.

item.title: 挥金如土 - Reckless extravagance
item.description: The idiom 挥金如土 (huī jīn rú tǔ) is commonly used in classical Chinese literature to describe someone who spends money with reckless abandon, treating it as if it were mere dirt. This expression is derived from the Northern Song Dynasty, specifically from a text by Mao Pang (毛滂) in his 'Funeral Oration for Zheng Tinghui' (祭郑庭诲文). Zheng Tinghui was known for his generous nature and indifference to wealth, often spending money freely without concern. The idiom breaks down into 挥 (huī, to wield or scatter), 金 (jīn, gold), 如 (rú, like), and 土 (tǔ, earth or soil). In modern usage, it carries a negative connotation, suggesting wasteful extravagance and a lack of financial prudence.

item.title: 回头是岸 - Repent and be saved
item.description: Commonly used in classical Chinese literature, the idiom 回头是岸 (huí tóu shì àn) originates from Buddhist teachings. It conveys the idea that those lost in the 'sea of suffering' (苦海, kǔ hǎi) can find salvation by turning back (回头, huí tóu) to reach the 'shore' (是岸, shì àn). This metaphorical shore represents redemption and a new beginning. The phrase emphasizes self-awareness and repentance as paths to escape from wrongdoing or a troubled life. While the idiom's roots lie in Buddhist philosophy, it has been widely adopted in Chinese literature to encourage individuals to correct their mistakes and seek a better path. In modern usage, it serves as a reminder that it's never too late to change one's ways and find a way out of difficult situations.

item.title: 见义勇为 - Courageous moral action
item.description: Commonly used in classical Chinese, the idiom 见义勇为 (jiàn yì yǒng wéi) describes the act of seeing (见, jiàn) a righteous deed (义, yì) and courageously (勇, yǒng) acting (为, wéi) upon it. The phrase emphasizes the moral character of individuals who, without any legal obligation, step forward to protect others or uphold justice, often at personal risk. This idiom is deeply rooted in Confucian values, highlighting the importance of righteousness and bravery. In modern times, it is used to commend those who take bold actions to prevent harm or injustice, reflecting a societal appreciation for selfless acts of valor.

item.title: 脚踏两船 - Indecision, double-dealing
item.description: Commonly used in classical Chinese, the idiom 脚踏两船 (jiǎo tà liǎng chuán) literally translates to 'one foot in two boats.' This vivid imagery paints a picture of someone attempting to stand with a foot in each of two boats, a precarious position that risks falling into the water. The characters break down as follows: 脚 (jiǎo) means 'foot,' 踏 (tà) means 'step on,' 两 (liǎng) means 'two,' and 船 (chuán) means 'boat.' In modern usage, this idiom describes a situation where someone is trying to maintain two conflicting interests or relationships simultaneously, often leading to instability or failure. It serves as a cautionary metaphor about the dangers of indecision and the importance of commitment to a single path.

item.title: 节外生枝 - Unnecessary complications
item.description: The idiom 节外生枝 (jié wài shēng zhī) is commonly used in classical Chinese literature. It originates from the teachings of the prominent Song dynasty scholar Zhu Xi (朱熹), who emphasized the importance of focused and methodical study. Zhu Xi criticized the tendency to overcomplicate matters by introducing unnecessary complications, likening it to branches (枝, zhī) growing from a joint (节, jié) where they are not needed. This imagery suggests that adding extra elements can hinder progress. In modern usage, 节外生枝 describes situations where new problems arise unexpectedly, often due to overthinking or meddling, which complicates the resolution of the original issue.

item.title: 锦上添花 - Enhance existing beauty
item.description: The idiom 锦上添花 (jǐn shàng tiān huā) originates from a story involving Xue Rengui during the Tang Dynasty. Xue Rengui, once impoverished, was aided by Wang Maosheng, who provided him with necessities. Later, Xue Rengui achieved great success and was ennobled as the King of Two Liao. When local officials and gentry presented him with gifts, he remarked that their gestures were like 'adding flowers to brocade' (锦, brocade; 花, flower), emphasizing that Wang Maosheng's help during his hardships was far more valuable. This idiom highlights the idea that assistance during difficult times (雪中送炭, 'sending charcoal in snow') is more precious than embellishments during prosperity. In modern usage, it describes enhancing something already beautiful or successful.

item.title: 惊弓之鸟 - Fear from past trauma
item.description: This idiom, 惊弓之鸟 (jīng gōng zhī niǎo), originates from a story in the Warring States period, found in the text 《战国策》 (Zhàn Guó Cè). The tale involves a skilled archer named 更羸 (Gèng Léng) who, while with the King of Wei, claimed he could bring down a bird with just the sound of a bowstring. When a wounded bird flew by, 更羸 pulled the bowstring without an arrow, and the bird fell, frightened by the sound due to its previous injury. The characters break down as follows: 惊 (jīng, frightened), 弓 (gōng, bow), 之 (zhī, of), 鸟 (niǎo, bird). In modern usage, this idiom describes someone who is easily frightened or overly cautious due to past trauma or negative experiences.

item.title: 精雕细刻 - Attention to detail
item.description: The idiom 精雕细刻 (jīng diāo xì kè) is commonly used in classical Chinese to describe meticulous and detailed craftsmanship. Originally, it referred to the careful and precise carving of art pieces, emphasizing the artisan's dedication and skill. The characters 精 (jīng) and 雕 (diāo) denote 'exquisite' and 'carve,' while 细 (xì) and 刻 (kè) mean 'fine' and 'engrave.' Over time, this idiom has evolved to symbolize a thorough and conscientious approach to any task, not just in art but also in various fields of work. It highlights the importance of attention to detail and the pursuit of excellence. In modern usage, it is often applied to describe someone who approaches their work with care and precision, ensuring high-quality outcomes.

item.title: 居功自傲 - Pride leads to downfall
item.description: Commonly used in classical Chinese, the idiom 居功自傲 (jū gōng zì ào) warns against the dangers of arrogance following success. The phrase is composed of four characters: 居 (jū, to occupy), 功 (gōng, achievement), 自 (zì, self), and 傲 (ào, arrogance). It describes a person who becomes conceited due to their accomplishments, often leading to negative consequences. A historical example is the story of Xu You, who, despite his contributions, faced dire outcomes due to his arrogance. In modern usage, this idiom serves as a reminder to remain humble and cautious, even in the face of success, to avoid the pitfalls of pride.

item.title: 举世闻名 - Global recognition
item.description: Commonly used in classical Chinese, the idiom 举世闻名 (jǔ shì wén míng) describes something that is renowned worldwide. The phrase is composed of four characters: 举 (jǔ), meaning 'to lift' or 'to raise'; 世 (shì), meaning 'world'; 闻 (wén), meaning 'to hear' or 'to be known'; and 名 (míng), meaning 'name' or 'fame'. Together, they create the image of something being lifted up for the entire world to see and recognize. This idiom is often used to describe cultural landmarks, achievements, or individuals whose fame has spread across the globe. It emphasizes the idea of universal recognition and acclaim, highlighting the success and perseverance required to achieve such a status.

item.title: 举世瞩目 - Global attention
item.description: The idiom 举世瞩目 (jǔ shì zhǔ mù) is commonly used in classical Chinese literature to depict situations where an event or achievement draws the attention of the entire world. The phrase is composed of 举 (jǔ, all) and 瞩目 (zhǔ mù, gaze), together forming an image of global focus. Although its specific origin story is not well-documented, it is influenced by the Spring and Autumn period text 《国语·晋语》, where the phrase '国人之瞩目于我也' suggests the idea of being under the watchful eyes of many. This idiom is often used in modern contexts to describe significant accomplishments or events that garner widespread international attention, such as groundbreaking scientific discoveries or major political changes.

item.title: 开卷有益 - Reading brings wisdom
item.description: The idiom 开卷有益 (kāi juàn yǒu yì) originates from the Northern Song Dynasty, associated with Emperor Taizong (宋太宗). He was known for his love of reading and emphasized the importance of books in governance. Despite his busy schedule, he committed to reading three volumes of the massive encyclopedia 《太平总类》 daily, believing that 'opening a book is beneficial.' This dedication to learning inspired his officials and became a cultural norm. The characters 开 (kāi, open), 卷 (juàn, book), 有 (yǒu, have), and 益 (yì, benefit) together convey the idea that reading always brings benefits. Today, this idiom encourages people to read diligently, emphasizing that knowledge gained from books enhances wisdom and understanding.

item.title: 苦口婆心 - Patient, sincere advice
item.description: The idiom 苦口婆心 (kǔ kǒu pó xīn) is commonly used in classical Chinese to describe the act of advising someone with sincere and patient intentions. The phrase combines 苦口 (kǔ kǒu), meaning 'bitter mouth,' and 婆心 (pó xīn), meaning 'old woman's heart,' to convey the idea of offering advice that may be difficult to hear but is given with a caring and compassionate heart. This idiom is often used to emphasize the importance of listening to well-intentioned advice, even if it is not immediately pleasant. It reflects the cultural value of 'good medicine tastes bitter,' suggesting that honest and constructive criticism, though hard to accept, is ultimately beneficial. In modern usage, it highlights the virtue of patience and kindness in guiding others.

item.title: 快马加鞭 - Accelerate progress
item.description: The idiom 快马加鞭 (kuài mǎ jiā biān) is commonly used in classical Chinese literature to describe the act of urging a fast horse (快马) to go even faster by applying the whip (加鞭). This vivid imagery captures the essence of accelerating progress or intensifying efforts when already moving quickly. The phrase first appeared in the Song dynasty, notably in the works of poets like Lu You and Wang Anshi, who used it to convey urgency and the need for relentless advancement. In modern contexts, this idiom is frequently employed to encourage individuals or organizations to expedite their efforts, especially in competitive or time-sensitive situations.

item.title: 旷日持久 - Wasteful delay
item.description: The idiom 旷日持久 (kuàng rì chí jiǔ) originates from a historical context during the Warring States period, as recorded in the Western Han text 《战国策》 (Zhànguó Cè). The story involves the state of Zhao, which faced an invasion from the state of Yan. Zhao's leaders, despite advice to the contrary, sought external military aid by ceding territory to the state of Qi. This decision led to a prolonged (旷日, kuàng rì) and enduring (持久, chí jiǔ) conflict, draining Zhao's resources. The idiom highlights the importance of strategic foresight and warns against engaging in protracted endeavors that waste time and resources. In modern usage, it describes situations where efforts are unnecessarily extended, often leading to inefficiency.

item.title: 劳苦功高 - Success through perseverance
item.description: The idiom 劳苦功高 (láo kǔ gōng gāo) originates from the historical text 《史记》 (Shǐjì), written by Sima Qian during the Western Han Dynasty. It is associated with the story of Liu Bang, who later became the first emperor of the Han Dynasty. During the tumultuous period following the fall of the Qin Dynasty, Liu Bang's forces captured the capital, Xianyang, before Xiang Yu, another powerful leader. Despite Liu Bang's significant contributions and hardships (劳苦, hard work), he was not immediately rewarded, which led to tensions with Xiang Yu. The idiom highlights the concept of achieving great success (功高, high achievements) through perseverance and hard work. In modern usage, it is used to commend individuals who have made significant contributions through their diligent efforts.

item.title: 老生常谈 - Obvious yet wise advice
item.description: The idiom 老生常谈 (lǎo shēng cháng tán) originates from the Three Kingdoms period, specifically from the story of Guan Lu, a well-known diviner. Guan Lu was summoned by two influential officials, He Yan and Deng Yang, to predict their futures. When Guan Lu advised them to act with virtue and humility, Deng Yang dismissed his words as 'old scholar's common talk' (老生常谈), implying they were unoriginal and mundane. Despite the dismissal, Guan Lu's advice proved prophetic when both officials met a tragic end due to their arrogance. The idiom breaks down into 老 (lǎo, old), 生 (shēng, scholar), 常 (cháng, common), and 谈 (tán, talk). In modern usage, it refers to statements or advice that seem obvious or unoriginal but often carry significant truth and wisdom.

item.title: 乐此不疲 - Passionate engagement
item.description: The idiom 乐此不疲 (lè cǐ bù pí) originates from the historical account of Emperor Guangwu of Han (汉光武帝, Hàn Guāngwǔ Dì), Liu Xiu (刘秀, Liú Xiù), as recorded in the 'Book of the Later Han' (《后汉书》, Hòu Hàn Shū) by Fan Ye (范晔, Fàn Yè). Emperor Guangwu was known for his tireless dedication to governance, working from dawn until late at night. When his son expressed concern for his health, suggesting he should take time to rest, Liu Xiu replied, 'I enjoy this and do not feel tired' (我自乐此，不为疲也, wǒ zì lè cǐ, bù wéi pí yě). The idiom breaks down as follows: 乐 (lè) means 'enjoy,' 此 (cǐ) means 'this,' 不 (bù) means 'not,' and 疲 (pí) means 'tired.' In modern usage, it describes someone who is so passionate about an activity that they do not feel exhausted by it.

item.title: 里应外合 - Coordinated collaboration
item.description: The idiom 里应外合 (lǐ yìng wài hé) is commonly used in classical Chinese to describe a strategic collaboration where internal and external forces work together to achieve a common goal. The phrase is composed of four characters: 里 (lǐ, inside), 应 (yìng, respond), 外 (wài, outside), and 合 (hé, cooperate). This idiom is often associated with military tactics, where an internal faction coordinates with an external force to launch a successful attack. Its earliest known usage is in the Yuan dynasty play '豫让吞炭' by Yang Zi, which describes a scenario where internal and external forces collaborate to flood an enemy army. In modern contexts, 里应外合 is used to describe any situation where internal and external parties collaborate effectively, such as in business or political strategies.

item.title: 良辰美景 - Cherish perfect moments
item.description: Commonly used in classical Chinese literature, the idiom 良辰美景 (liáng chén měi jǐng) evokes the dual beauty of time and scenery. The phrase first appeared in the Southern Dynasties, attributed to poet Xie Lingyun in his preface to a collection of poems. 良 (liáng) means 'good' or 'beautiful,' and 辰 (chén) refers to 'time' or 'moment,' while 美 (měi) describes 'beautiful,' and 景 (jǐng) means 'scenery.' Together, they paint a picture of an idyllic moment where time and surroundings are in perfect harmony. In modern usage, this idiom is often employed to describe moments when both the environment and one's mood are at their best, encouraging appreciation of life's fleeting beauty.

item.title: 两全其美 - Win-win outcome
item.description: The idiom 两全其美 (liǎng quán qí měi) originates from a story in the Yuan Dynasty, found in the play 《连环计》. It tells of Wang Yun, a court official, who devised a plan to eliminate the tyrant Dong Zhuo. He used his adopted daughter, Diao Chan, to create discord between Dong Zhuo and his formidable foster son, Lü Bu. By promising Diao Chan to both men, Wang Yun ensured that they would turn against each other, ultimately leading to Dong Zhuo's demise. The idiom literally means 'both complete and beautiful' (两, both; 全, complete; 其, its; 美, beautiful), and it describes a situation where a solution benefits all parties involved. In modern usage, it refers to achieving a win-win outcome, where both sides are satisfied.

item.title: 临渊羡鱼 - Act, don't just dream
item.description: The idiom 临渊羡鱼 (lín yuān xiàn yú) originates from the Western Han dynasty text 《淮南子·说林训》. The story is about a person who stands by a river, admiring the fish (羡鱼, xiàn yú) but does nothing to catch them. Instead of merely longing for the fish, it is wiser to return home and weave a net (结网, jié wǎng) to catch them. This idiom highlights the importance of taking practical action rather than indulging in idle wishes. The characters 临 (lín) means 'approach' or 'stand by', 渊 (yuān) means 'deep pool', 羡 (xiàn) means 'admire', and 鱼 (yú) means 'fish'. In modern usage, it serves as a reminder to focus on actionable steps rather than just aspirations.

item.title: 流芳百世 - Enduring good reputation
item.description: The idiom 流芳百世 (liú fāng bǎi shì) originates from a story involving Huan Wen (桓温), a prominent general during the Eastern Jin Dynasty. Despite his high status and military achievements, Huan Wen harbored ambitions of becoming emperor. In a moment of reflection, he expressed his desire for his name to be remembered positively for generations, saying, 'If a man cannot leave a good name to be praised for a hundred generations, should he not at least leave a bad name to be cursed for ten thousand years?' (流芳百世, 遗臭万年). The characters 流 (liú, flow), 芳 (fāng, fragrance), 百 (bǎi, hundred), and 世 (shì, generations) together convey the idea of a good reputation enduring through time. Today, the idiom is used to praise individuals whose virtuous deeds and contributions to society ensure their legacy is celebrated long after their passing.

item.title: 龙飞凤舞 - Dynamic elegance
item.description: The idiom 龙飞凤舞 (lóng fēi fèng wǔ) is commonly used in classical Chinese literature to describe a scene of dynamic and elegant movement, often likened to the majestic flight of dragons (龙, lóng) and the graceful dance of phoenixes (凤, fèng). While there is no specific historical origin or story tied to this idiom, it paints a vivid picture of vitality and beauty. In modern usage, it can refer to anything that is lively, spirited, and artistically impressive, such as calligraphy or a performance.

item.title: 龙腾虎跃 - Energetic pursuit of success
item.description: The idiom 龙腾虎跃 (lóng téng hǔ yuè) is commonly used in classical Chinese to evoke the imagery of a dragon soaring and a tiger leaping. Although its specific origin is not tied to a single historical text, the imagery of dragons and tigers has long been associated with power, vitality, and dynamic action in Chinese culture. Dragons (龙, lóng) symbolize strength and auspiciousness, while tigers (虎, hǔ) represent courage and ferocity. Together, they create a vivid picture of energetic and vigorous movement. In modern usage, this idiom is often used to describe situations where people or groups are actively pursuing success with great enthusiasm and perseverance.

item.title: 落落大方 - Confident and charming
item.description: Commonly used in classical Chinese literature, the idiom 落落大方 (luò luò dà fāng) describes a person whose demeanor is naturally poised and graceful. The characters 落落 (luò luò) suggest a sense of ease and composure, while 大方 (dà fāng) conveys elegance and generosity. This idiom is often used to praise individuals who exhibit confidence and charm in their interactions, without any trace of awkwardness or pretension. In modern contexts, it is applied to describe people who are articulate and self-assured, whether in public speaking or social settings, embodying a refined and approachable character.

item.title: 马到功成 - Swift success
item.description: The idiom 马到功成 (mǎ dào gōng chéng) is commonly used in classical Chinese literature to describe achieving success swiftly and efficiently. The phrase literally translates to 'the horse arrives, success is achieved,' painting a vivid image of a victorious return from battle as soon as the horse reaches its destination. The idiom first appeared in the Yuan dynasty play 楚昭公 (Chǔ Zhāo Gōng) by Zheng Tingyu, where it was used to express the hope for a quick and triumphant military campaign. In modern contexts, this idiom is often used to wish someone success in new endeavors, such as business ventures or projects, and is frequently paired with 旗开得胜 (qí kāi dé shèng), meaning 'to win the first battle.'

item.title: 满面春风 - Radiant happiness
item.description: Commonly used in classical Chinese literature, the idiom 满面春风 (mǎn miàn chūn fēng) paints a vivid picture of a person whose face is glowing with happiness and warmth, much like the gentle and refreshing breeze of spring (春风, spring breeze). The phrase originates from a line in the Song dynasty poem 《沁园春》 by Cheng Jiezai (程节斋), where it describes a person exuding joy and amiability (满面, full face; 春风, spring breeze). In modern usage, this idiom is often employed to describe someone who is visibly cheerful and approachable, often due to good news or personal success.

item.title: 盲人瞎马 - Reckless action
item.description: This idiom, 盲人瞎马 (máng rén xiā mǎ), originates from a story in the classical Chinese text 《世说新语》 (Shì Shuō Xīn Yǔ) by Liu Yiqing during the Southern Dynasties period. The story involves three Eastern Jin scholars, Yin Zhongkan, Huan Xuan, and Gu Kaizhi, who were engaged in a storytelling contest. Gu Kaizhi described a perilous scenario: 'A blind man (盲人, máng rén) riding a blind horse (瞎马, xiā mǎ) approaching a deep pond at midnight.' This vivid imagery highlights the extreme danger of acting without awareness or foresight. In modern usage, the idiom serves as a caution against reckless behavior and emphasizes the importance of careful planning and awareness before taking action.

item.title: 眉目传情 - Non-verbal affection
item.description: The idiom 眉目传情 (méi mù chuán qíng) is commonly used in classical Chinese literature to describe the subtle and non-verbal communication of emotions, particularly romantic feelings, through the eyes and facial expressions. This expression first appeared in the Yuan dynasty play 'The Romance of the Western Chamber' (《西厢记》) by Wang Shifu, where it depicted the silent exchange of affection between the characters Cui Yingying and Zhang Sheng. The characters 眉 (méi, eyebrows) and 目 (mù, eyes) emphasize the role of facial expressions, while 传 (chuán, transmit) and 情 (qíng, feelings) highlight the conveyance of emotions. In modern usage, this idiom is often employed to describe situations where people communicate their feelings without words, relying instead on the expressive power of their gaze and facial nuances.

item.title: 门可罗雀 - Abandoned by fair-weather friends
item.description: The idiom 门可罗雀 (mén kě luó què) originates from the 'Records of the Grand Historian' (史记) by Sima Qian, a foundational text of Chinese historiography. It recounts the story of two Han dynasty officials, Ji An and Zheng Zhuang, who were known for their integrity and straightforwardness. Despite their virtues, they fell out of favor and were dismissed from their positions. Once influential, their homes were bustling with visitors, but after losing power, their residences became so deserted that one could set up nets to catch sparrows (罗雀). The characters 门 (mén, door), 可 (kě, can), 罗 (luó, net), and 雀 (què, sparrow) vividly depict this scene.
